coredata.dc:title: Rapid and universal detection of ovalbumin based on N,O,P-co-doped carbon dots-fluorescence resonance energy transfer technology
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Xuan
Sheng, Long
Yu, Yisha
Ma, Meihu
Cai, Zhaoxia
Huang, Xi
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), a near-field energy transfer from a fluorescent donor to a fluorescent acceptor within close proximity, has been widely used in biosensing. In this study, a rapid and universal sensor for the detection of ovalbumin (OVA) using nitrogen, oxygen, phosphoric co-doped CDs (NOP-CDs) and graphene oxide (GO) was established based on the FRET technology. First, novel NOP-CDs peak at 443 nm were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using egg powder and phosphorus acid. Then, in the absence of OVA, the fluorescence of OVA-antibody modified NOP-CDs was quenched by GO via electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interaction due to the FRET effect. The introduced OVA competed to displace the GO by special binding with the anti-OVA, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence. This sensor displayed a linear response to OVA from 0.5 to 15 μg/mL with a detection limit of 153 ng/mL and was successfully applied to the detection of the OVA sample with recoveries ranging from 99.25 to 118.90%. In summary, the green and easy-to-synthesize nano-material was used in this method, the accurate detection results, and the simple process exhibited the great potential of this method for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Ovalbumin
Nitrogen
Oxygen
Phosphorus co-doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NOP-CDs, un-doped −CDs and GO
Detection of OVA based on FRET
Specificity of the sensor for OVA and analytical performance
Determination of OVA in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Excellent Youth Foundation of Hubei Province Natural Science Foundation
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Excellent Youth Foundation of Hubei Province Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018CFA073 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Project code No. 2662015PY080). Xuan Fu received his bachelor in food science and engineering from Wu Han Polytechnic University in 2015. He is pursuing his MS in food science from Huazhong Agriculture University. He is currently studying in the area of fluorescence biosensors based on nanomaterial. Long Sheng received his PhD from Jiangnan University in 2015. He is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a lecturer. He is currently working toward the activity and function of egg proteins. YishaYu received her bachelor from Huazhong Agriculture University in 2017. Her research is focus on the food science. Meihu Ma received his PhD from Hunan Agriculture University, ChangSha, in 2005. He is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a professor. His research is focused on animal product processing and quality testing. Zhaoxia Cai received her PhD in analytical chemistry from Nankai University in 2007. She is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a professor. She studied in the area of light analytical chemistry. She is currently working toward fluorescence biosensors based on nanomaterial. Xi Huang received her PhD in college of chemistry from Central China Normal University in 2009. She is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as an associate professor. She is currently working toward the activity and function of egg proteins.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sensing process for OVA based on FRET.

(A) TEM images of the NOP-CDs, inset shows the HRTEM image of the NOP-CDs. (B) The corresponding size distribution of the NOP-CDs. (C) AFM images of the NOP-CDs (D) Height-profiles analysis along the corresponding lines.

(A) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of C-dots. Inset: images of the C-dots dispersion under visible (left) and UV light (right). (B) DLS spectrum of NOP-CDs. (C) The XRD patterns of un-doped-CDs. (D) The XRD patterns of NOP-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of NOP-CDs-anti-OVA (a) and NOP-CDs (b). (B) DLS spectrum of NOP-CDs (a), GO (b), NOP-CDs-GO (c) and NOP-CDs-anti-OVA-OVA-GO (d). (C) Fluorescence spectrum of NOP-CDs (a) and the Absorb Spectrum GO (b).
(A) Entire XPS scanning spectrum of the C-dots. XPS survey spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C) O 1s (D) P 2p (E) of the NOP-CDs. (F) FTIR spectrum of NOP-CDs and un-doped-CDs.
FI of the detection system as a function of pH (A), temperature (B) and time(C).

(A) Fluorescence quenching spectra of the system at different concentration of GO. The GO concentrations from a to h are 0, 20, 60, 80, 100, 300, 400, and 500 μg/mL. (B) Quenching efficiency measured at different GO concentration. (C) Fluorescence recovery spectra of the system upon the addition of various OVA concentrations from 0 to 1000 ng/mL. (D) Linear relationship for OVA concentrations and fluorescence intensity changes.
FI response ((Fr-Fq)/Fq) of the detection system toward various interfering substances and OVA Sample.
Results for OVA determination in synthesis sample.
Determination of OVA in actual samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: NMa indicates no mention in the paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery and RSD of OVA detection.

head.ce:title: Rapid and universal detection of ovalbumin based on N,O,P-co-doped carbon dots-fluorescence resonance energy transfer technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Key Laboratory of Egg Processing, Ministry of Agricultrue and Rural Affairs, Wuhan, Hubei 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
Ministry of Agricultrue and Rural Affairs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Technology of Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, Hubei Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), a near-field energy transfer from a fluorescent donor to a fluorescent acceptor within close proximity, has been widely used in biosensing. In this study, a rapid and universal sensor for the detection of ovalbumin (OVA) using nitrogen, oxygen, phosphoric co-doped CDs (NOP-CDs) and graphene oxide (GO) was established based on the FRET technology. First, novel NOP-CDs peak at 443 nm were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using egg powder and phosphorus acid. Then, in the absence of OVA, the fluorescence of OVA-antibody modified NOP-CDs was quenched by GO via electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interaction due to the FRET effect. The introduced OVA competed to displace the GO by special binding with the anti-OVA, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence. This sensor displayed a linear response to OVA from 0.5 to 15 μg/mL with a detection limit of 153 ng/mL and was successfully applied to the detection of the OVA sample with recoveries ranging from 99.25 to 118.90%. In summary, the green and easy-to-synthesize nano-material was used in this method, the accurate detection results, and the simple process exhibited the great potential of this method for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovalbumin (OVA), a major protein component in egg white, constitutes approximately 54% of the total proteins [1]. As the most abundant protein in egg white, the propriety of OVA affects the properties of egg white in the food industry. The native OVA will progressively change into stable OVA during the storage or hatch period [2]. With increasing storage time, the OVA content gradually declines with the reduction of the emulsifying stability as well as the foaming and gelling ability [3,4], causing the alternation of the egg white properties [5,6]. Huang’s work [3] has also shown that the form of stable OVA will reduce the Haugh unit and yolk index and increase the albumen pH. Therefore, the OVA content is always used as the reference index for the evaluation of the egg quality and freshness.

Since food allergies have been recognized as a main focus of recent health research [7,8], development of analysis methods can efficiently reduce the health hazards resulting from allergens [9]. OVA, as the main allergen in egg, should be detected [1]. In view of the conditions above, it is urgent to develop a method for the rapid and accurate determination of the OVA content.

Many methods have been developed to determine protein, such as the rolling circle amplification-procedure enhanced immunoassay [10], enzyme-linked immunosensor assays (ELISA) [11] and immunochromatographic Strip [12]. However, many of these show some drawbacks such as insufficiently high sensitivity, long time requirements, complicated methods and high cost. The method based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technique has been widely used in biosensing due to its high sensitivity, reliability, fast and low cost.

In the FRET-based detection system, an outstanding donor-acceptor pair with favorable optical properties can considerably enhance the FRET efficiency and performance, resulting in the improvement of the sensor sensitivity. Recently, fluorescence nanoparticles such as semiconductor quantum dots [13] and carbon dots (CDs) [14] show good photostability, large stokes shift, narrow emission, and broad excitation, which could provide a significant enhancement for the detection sensitivity. However, the poor photobleaching resistance, low chemical stability and relatively short fluorescent lifetime greatly restrict the use of CDs in a broad range of FRET-based applications.

Nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus co-doped CDs (NOP-CDs), a newly emerging form of CDs, have attracted tremendous attention in various fields. Compared to the traditional CDs, the fluorescence properties, water solubility and biocompatibility of NOP-CDs are significantly enhanced by the doping of nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorus. [15,16]. In addition, groups such as −NH2 and −COOH afford more binding sites for the conjugation with antibodies, and these advantages further enhance the FRET efficiency of the system. Many nanoparticles with special optical quenching properties such as gold nanoparticles, metal dichalcogenides, and graphene oxide (GO) have been used as acceptors. Among these, GO shows excellent acceptor potential because of its excellent quench ability arising from its sp2 electronic structure and broad absorption bands [17,18]. In addition, the 2D nanosheet structure with a high surface to volume ratio and easy functionalization with biomolecules also makes GO widely used in optically based applications [19].

In this study, the novel NOP-CDs with narrow size were synthesized in a one-pot process from hydrothermal egg white powder and phosphoric acid. Then, a sensor for the specific detection of OVA was developed based on FRET. Typically, NOP-CDs and GO were designed as the energy donor and acceptor, respectively, for the FRET system. The bright fluorescence from the NOP-CDs was quenched in the presence of GO due to the π-π stacking and electrical attraction between NOP-CDs and GO (“turn off” state) [20,21]. After the addition of OVA to the NOP-CDs-anti-OVA-GO system, NOP-CDs-anti-OVA were detached from the GO surface and attached to OVA because of immunoreactions, creating a “turn on” state [22]. This method shows many advantages such as the use of cheap and green materials, no requirement for multiple time-consuming incubations, separation or washing steps and rapid execution. The sensor was demonstrated to possess high sensitivity and would create new opportunities for the synthesis and application of NOP-CDs, the whole detection could be finished within 2 h, which was significant less than the voltammetric [23], the ELISA [24] or the immuno-SERS [25].

OVA and avidin were purchased from YuanYe Company (Wuhan. China). Anti-OVA (Rabbit polyclonal to Ovalbumin) was purchased from abcam Company (Wuhan. China). NaCl, KCl, NaOH, glucose, lactose, Na2HPO4, and NaH2PO4 were acquired from GuoYao Company (Beijing, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was acquired from the Sinopharm Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was acquired from the Aladdin Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Glycine, cysteine, glutathione, aspartic acid, L-serine, and tyrosine were purchased from the Biosharp Company (Shanghai, China). Graphite powder was acquired from Xianfeng nanometer Mstar Technology Company (Nanjing, China). Egg products (egg white powder and yolk powder) were purchased from the Kangde Biological Products Company (Nantong, China).
All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water generated by an ELGA Purelab Option (UK) instrument with an electric resistance ≥18.2 MΩ.

OVA was prepared as a 1 mg/mL stock solution and diluted to 0.01 mg/mL as the working solution. Anti-OVA was prepared at 100 μg/mL. All solutions were stored at 4 °C. Phosphate-buffered solutions (PBS) with different pH were prepared by mixing 0.01 M Na2HPO4 and 0.01 M NaH2PO4, according to the prescribed proportions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NOP-CDs, un-doped −CDs and GO
ce:section.ce:para: NOP-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal decomposition method using egg white powder and phosphoric acid. Typically, 0.3 g of egg white powder was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and 5 mL of 2 M H3PO4 was added. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-autoclave (Yantu instrument company Ltd, Nanjing, China). Then, the solution was heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Egg white powder (0.3 g) was dispersed in 15 mL of ultrapure water, and reacted at the same situation to synthesis the un-doped CDs. The product was cooled to room temperature and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The pH was neutralized with NaOH (1 M). Finally, the NOP-CDs were purified by ethyl alcohol.
GO were prepared from nature graphite powder by the Hummers method [26].

To obtain the microstructure and morphology of the samples, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were performed. TEM images and HRTEM images were obtained using a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. AFM images were obtained using SPM9700 Atomic Force microscope (Shimadzum, Japan). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) of NOP-CDs was characterized by a laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, United Kingdom) at 25 °C. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) study was carried out using a Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker AXS, Germany) with a graphite monochromator using Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a GENESIS X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (EDAX Inc, America) using AlKa X-ray radiation. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nexus 470 FTIR spectrometer (NICOLET, America) after pelletizing a homogenized powder of the synthesized nanoparticles and KBr with the resolution of 2 cm−1 in the 500–4000 cm−1 range.

The fluorescence spectra of NOP-CDs were recorded using a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set as 3 nm and 10 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the NOP-CDs-anti-OVA was marked as Fq and F0 in the presence or absence of OVA, respectively. The absorption spectrum of GO was obtained using a Nanodrop 2000C (Thermo scientific, America) UV–vis spectrophotometer.

NOP-CDs-anti-OVA were prepared according to a reported method with some modifications [27]. Activation of the NOP-CDs were carried out by incubating 3 mL of NOP-CDs (0.13 mg/mL) with 1.4 mL of EDC-NHS solution (12 μg EDC and 12 μg NHS in 14 mL of 1 mM PBS, pH 7.0) for 30 min at room temperature with constant stirring. Then, activated NOP-CDs were incubated with 25 μL anti-OVA solution (100 μg/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C to form NOP-CDs-anti-OVA conjugation. In this step, NOP-CDs and anti-OVA were conjugated through strong covalent bonds. The solution described above was diluted to 22 mL and stored at 4 °C.

One milliliter NOP-CDs-anti-OVA (1.13 μg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL of different concentrations of GO (0–500 μg/mL) in 1 mL PBS (1 mM, pH 7.0), and then, 7 mL ultrapure water was added and incubated for 30 min to investigate the quenching efficiency between GO and NOP-CDs.

One milliliter (100 μg/mL) GO was mixed with 0.5 mL diluted NOP-CDs-anti-OVA (The concentration of anti-OVA was 1.13 μg/mL) in 1 mL PBS (1 mM, pH 7.0) and incubated for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of different concentrations of OVA (0–50 μg/mL) and 6.5 mL water were added and mixed, and after incubation for 2 h, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

Egg white powder, yolk powder and egg white liquid were chosen as test samples to detect the OVA concentration because they are the raw materials frequently used in the food industry. A 100 mg sample was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixtures were then centrifuged at 5000 rpm (3–30N, Sigma, Germany) for 15 min at 4 °C to deplete the high-abundance proteins. The supernatants were collected and diluted as food samples for future detection. The concentration of OVA in the food sample solutions was diluted until it was within the linear range of the calibration curve established in this study.

The size and microstructure of the NOP-CDs were characterized by TEM images, HRTEM images and AFM images. The TEM images (Fig. 1 A–B) revealed that the samples were dispersed very well, with average particle sizes of approximately 3.1 ± 0.6 nm. The HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 1A) showed that all of the CDs exhibited identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. The AFM images (Fig. 1C–D) showed that the particle height of NOP-CDs was about 3–4 nm.

To reveal the optical properties of the NOP-CDs, the fluorescence excitation, emission spectra and images of the NOP-CDs were studied. Fig. 2 A shows that the NOP-CDs suspension was light yellow, transparent and emitted bright blue light under UV illumination. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-CDs were 360 nm and 443 nm, respectively. The emission spectra were characteristically narrow and symmetric, indicating the excellent optical properties of the NOP-CDs. The quantum yield (QY) of NOP-CDs was measured to be 11.1% (54% by quinine sulfate as a reference), the QY was enhanced, compare to the QY of un-doped CDs (6.8%).

The DLS result (Fig. 2B) showed that NOP-CDs were stable without any aggregation and the average hydrodynamic particle size of NOP-CDs was smaller than 1 nm, indicating the good water solubility of NOP-CDs. The average hydrodynamic particle size of NOP-CDs was smaller than size measured by TEM and AFM, and the results similar to the published literature [28]. To reveal the relationship between optical properties and the structure, the XRD patterns of both un-doped-CDs and NOP-CDs were exhibited in Fig. 2C and 2D. In the case of un-doped-CDs, a broad and asymmetrical diffraction peak was detected at 20° as 2θ value, which indicated the amorphous disordered structure of un-doped-CDs. While the diffraction pattern of NOP-CDs contained a set of notable sharp peaks. The results revealed the formation of a highly ordered crystalline structure in the NOP-CDs (Fig. 2D), which may be responsible for the enhancement of the QY of the NOP-CDs [29]. To identify the surface composition of NOP-CDs, XPS and FTIR spectra were recorded. As seen from Fig. 3 A, the XPS spectrum of NOP-CDs exhibited five peaks at 133.269, 193.125, 285.019, 400.169 and 531.669 eV corresponding to P 2p, P 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [30]. The corresponding relative contents were calculated to be approximately 24.19% (C), 13.99% (N), 59.44% (O) and 3.37% (P), endowing the materials with high solubility, biocompatibility, and additional binding sites. Fig. 3B–E display the high-resolution spectra of C1s, N 1s, O 1s, and P 2p, and a series of N, O, P characteristic peaks could be found after deconvoluting the original data, such as the P-C (133.9 eV), CN/CO (286.1 eV), COOH (288.2 eV) pyridinic, amino, pyronic N (400.0 eV, 400.8 eV, 401.8 eV) and CO (531.5 eV) peaks [31–33]. The XPS data was further corroborated by the FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3F, several N, O, and P characteristic peaks were found in NOP-CDs, such as −NH2 (532 cm−1), POC (989 cm−1), P = O (1130 cm−1), CN (1600 cm−1), CO (1620 cm−1), P-OH (a broad peak between 2700 cm−1 and 2000 cm−1) and NH (3440 cm−1). Furthermore, peaks at 989 cm‐1, 1130 cm−1 and the peak of P-OH (broad peak between 2700 and 2000 cm−1) were absent in un-doped-CDs [34,35]. Both XPS and FTIR results showed that the NOP-CDs contained a high amount of hydrophilic groups (such as −NH2, −OH, CO and PO4 3−), which endowed the NOP-CDs with good hydrophilic properties [36]. The abundance of carboxyl groups and amino groups offered modification sites on the surface for coupling the NOP-CDs with antibodies [37]. In addition, phosphorous may come into being substitutional defects in diamond sp3 thin film, serve as an n-type donor and therefore tune the optical properties of NOP-CDs [38].

The working principle of this sensing system is shown in Scheme 1 . First, the amino groups on anti-OVA facilitated the binding among the anti-OVA and NOP-CDs by covalent bonding. Upon conjugation of OVA onto NOP-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of NOP-CDs was slightly enhanced (Fig. 4 A), indicating low agglomeration and fine dispersion in the labeled process [39]. In the absence of OVA, NOP-CDs-anti-OVA were absorbed on the GO surface through electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions, bringing GO (donor) and NOP-CDs (acceptor) close to each other. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, GO showed broad absorption, and the absorption spectrum of GO overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectra of NOP-CDs. The broad absorption of GO and narrow emission spectra of NOP-CDs used in this FRET provided a good basis for energy transfer. Consequently, when NOP-CDs were absorbed on the GO surface, the fluorescence of NOP-CDs was quenched by GO due to the FRET. After OVA was added, the specific interaction between the antibody and the antigen led to the form of NOP-CDs-anti-OVA/OVA structure, then effectively increased the distance between the NOP-CDs-anti-OVA and GO surface, thus impeding the FRET process and then leading to the restoration (recovery) of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4C, the operational principle of this method was well-confirmed by the DLS results.

To obtain a better response to the fluorescence probe, it is necessary to optimize the conditions that can significantly impact the detection of OVA such as the pH, temperature, and reaction time. The fluorescence intensity (FI) changes at different pH ranging from 5.0 to 9.0 were investigated due to the strong impact of pH on the activity of antibodies and OVA. Fig. 5 A shows that FI were rather low at alkaline and acidic conditions. As pH < 8.0, the change in FI became noticeable and then reached maximum at pH 6.0. Detection temperature is another critical aspect that affects the activity of antibody. Three detection temperatures (4 °C, 25 °C and 37 °C), which was common used in the detection based on the immune-interaction were chosen for the optimization test. It could be seen from Fig. 5B that the ΔF reached the highest value at 37 °C but was decreased significantly at relatively low temperatures (25 °C and 4 °C). Reaction time is the third key variable for this detection system. As shown in Fig. 5C, the FI of the system increased slightly with the reaction time increasing from 0 to 60 min and reached the maximum value at 60 min. Therefore, pH 6.0, reaction time of 60 min, and 37 °C were chosen as the reaction conditions in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OVA based on FRET
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of the energy receptor concentration on the detection system was investigated in this study due to the strong effect of the energy receptor on the fluorescent switch system. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with the addition of GO, the fluorescence intensity decreased dramatically, and maximum quenching was obtained when the GO concentration was 100 μg/mL (Fig. 6B), and the FRET efficiency reached to 42.3%. After the addition of OVA, the FI was recovered after the combination of NOP-CDs-anti-OVA and OVA. With the gradual addition of OVA, NOP-CDs-anti-OVA escaped from GO surface, causing the recovery of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6C). Thus, an immunofluorescence analysis method for OVA detection was developed. The fluorescence switch efficiency (Eswitch) of this method was also investigated following the method of Wang [40]. Here, Eswitch is calculated using Eqs. (1)–(3), where F0 and Fq are the FI of NOP-CDs-anti-OVA in the absence and presence of GO, and Fr is the FI of system in the presence of free OVA.
(1) Eswitch = Eq × Er
(2) Eq = (F0 − Fq)/F0
(3) Er = (Fr − Fq)/F0
Eq was calculated to be 0.512. Er was calculated to be 0.156. Therefore, the Eswitch value was 0.0799.

The linear relationship between the corresponding FI change (ΔF) and OVA concentrations (C) is shown in Fig. 6(D) (calibration graph), which can be expressed as ΔF = 15.548C + 33.232 with the regression coefficient (R2) of 0.9967. The lower limit of detection (LOD) of OVA is defined by the equation LOD = 3S0/K, where S0 is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 11) and K is the slope of the calibration curve. In this equation, the LOD was 153 ng/mL. The linear range was found to be 0.5–15 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the sensor for OVA and analytical performance
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specific recognition of the proposed method, some coexisting substances such as K+, glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), glutathione (Glu), avidin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and lysozyme (LYZ) (1 × 10−8M) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , the OVA detection concentration was 0.5–15 μg/mL, and all concentrations for the coexisting substances were significantly higher than the OVA detection concentration. The detection method was slightly influenced by the coexisting substances with the relative change within 5%, suggesting that the developed method had strong anti-interference ability.

Egg product is a complex system including salts, amino acids, proteins, and other substances. To further evaluate the interference of this method, substances in eggs were chosen to construct five synthetic samples to imitate a real sample. As shown in Table 1 , a given concentration of the synthetic samples had little influence on the detection system due to the strong interaction between OVA and anti-OVA, indicating that the detection of OVA could work in the complex system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OVA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The amounts of OVA in actual products (egg white powder, yolk powder, and egg white) were evaluated using this method. Certain amounts of samples were concentrated to remove insoluble proteins, and then, OVA content was determined by the developed method, to investigate the applicability. The analytical results for the actual samples are shown in Table 2 , and it can be seen that 302 mg and 61.8 mg OVA were detected from egg white powder and egg white, respectively, and no OVA was found in yolk powder. As shown in Table 3 , 99.25–118.90% recoveries were found with the relative standard deviation of less than 2% (n = 5), and these good results definitely demonstrated the accuracy and reliability of this FRET-based method for OVA detection in practical food samples.

In summary, the NOP-CDs could be obtained by a facile one-pot hydrothermal synthesis with egg white powder as the carbon source and H3PO4 as the oxidation agent; these NOP-CDs have high contents of oxygen (59.55%), nitrogen (13.99%) and phosphorus (3.37%) that endowed them with high solubility, biocompatibility, and a high number of binding sites. Furthermore, a FRET system constructed by NOP-CDs and GO was established. In the detection system, the fluorescence emission intensity of NOP-CDs-anti-OVA decreased significantly in the presence of GO due to the complementary overlaps of the emission bands of NOP-CDs with absorption bands of GO. With the addition of OVA, NOP-CDs-anti-OVA were released from GO surface and competitively combined with OVA leading to the recovery of FI. Under the optimal conditions, the linear calibration equation was obtained with a detection limit of 153 ng/mL. Therefore, this method was sensitive and selective for the detection of OVA. Moreover, our study may provide a new insight for the preparation of high nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus component CDs and a broader use of NOP-CDs in detection applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Excellent Youth Foundation of Hubei Province Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018CFA073) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Project code No. 2662015PY080).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on polypyrrole/carbon nanotubes composite for the detection of s-ovalbumin in egg white

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xuan Fu received his bachelor in food science and engineering from Wu Han Polytechnic University in 2015. He is pursuing his MS in food science from Huazhong Agriculture University. He is currently studying in the area of fluorescence biosensors based on nanomaterial.

Long Sheng received his PhD from Jiangnan University in 2015. He is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a lecturer. He is currently working toward the activity and function of egg proteins.
YishaYu received her bachelor from Huazhong Agriculture University in 2017. Her research is focus on the food science.

Meihu Ma received his PhD from Hunan Agriculture University, ChangSha, in 2005. He is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a professor. His research is focused on animal product processing and quality testing.

Zhaoxia Cai received her PhD in analytical chemistry from Nankai University in 2007. She is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as a professor. She studied in the area of light analytical chemistry. She is currently working toward fluorescence biosensors based on nanomaterial.

Xi Huang received her PhD in college of chemistry from Central China Normal University in 2009. She is currently working at Food Science and Technology College, Huazhong Agriculture University, as an associate professor. She is currently working toward the activity and function of egg proteins.
